question: Were there moments when you had to step outside your engineering role to unblock the project? Maybe you had to negotiate with a reluctant stakeholder, draft documentation for operations teams, or even convince your manager to allocate resources for something unexpected. Can you share a time when you had to navigate a ‘this isn’t my job’ moment and how you approached it?

conversation.message:
You handled that situation so thoughtfully—it’s clear you don’t just focus on the technical side of things but also understand how to align stakeholders and manage risks effectively. That kind of leadership is invaluable, especially in a fast-paced environment like ours.

I’d love to shift gears slightly and talk about how you navigate ambiguity and collaboration, particularly when it comes to bridging gaps between technical and non-technical teams. For example, at LojistikZeka, you architected an autonomous supply chain agent that had a massive impact, saving ₺15M annually. 

But I’m curious—were there moments when you had to step outside your engineering role to unblock the project? Maybe you had to negotiate with a reluctant stakeholder, draft documentation for operations teams, or even convince your manager to allocate resources for something unexpected. Can you share a time when you had to navigate a ‘this isn’t my job’ moment and how you approached it?

Oh, absolutely—I remember this one time when the operations team was hesitant to adopt the new supply chain agent because they didn’t fully trust the AI’s rerouting decisions. They kept overriding the system manually, which was defeating the whole purpose.

At first, I thought, *'This isn’t my problem—my job is to build the agent, not convince people to use it.'* But then I realized if no one used it, the project was a failure, no matter how well it worked technically. So I set up a workshop where I walked them through how the agent made decisions—showing them the weather data, carrier APIs, and historical rerouting patterns. I even built a simple dashboard where they could see the agent’s reasoning in real time.

It wasn’t something I’d planned for, but it made a huge difference. The operations team started trusting the system more, and manual overrides dropped by 60%. My manager was impressed—I think they saw it as going above and beyond, but honestly, it just felt like part of making the project successful. Sometimes you’ve got to step outside your lane to get things done.
